item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While compulsive effects like these would normally be a turn-off for me, something about the anomaly's effects of making you want to put this in your oral cavity, combined with the qualifications for qualify for Criterion A/B is so delightfully weird that I cannot help but love this. Upvote!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I misread and thought it said the people who meet the criteria were effected. That made the newspaper article a lot darker…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They're a task force local to New Jersey, and Super Bowl III was the only time the New York Jets have ever won (or even made it to) a Super Bowl.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article never clarifies what the "2061-Positive individuals" mentioned in the containment procedures are. Are "2061-Positive individuals" just individuals who are both susceptible to its effects and have been exposed to the SCP in question? If so, it'd be nice to have that connection spelled out a little more clearly, or rewording that bit of the containment procedures to avoid using the phrase in the first place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know I'm asking about an article that's 3+ years old and may not get a response, but it hasn't been asked yet so I figure it's worth a shot: What's the significance behind the MTF name, "Super Bowl Three"? I like MTF names, and their underhanded references, but I can't think of anything for this one, maybe because I'm not a football fan.

Also, this is a great article. Good job Kate!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That comes across as… really creepy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Jumping right to the absolute scummiest application of an SCP is a bad look.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe uh, just ignore this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is somehow oddly unnerving. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am personally glad it doesn't go the other route into the body.
That would be difficult to explain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is at least the second time you've said this. I think if you're going to make such an assertion, you should back it up with reasons you think it's funny/comedic/should be a -J. You're not obligated to explain your votes, but criticism that's short, cryptic and unhelpful does nobody any good.

Also, you're getting a little spammy - you don't need to post on every article you read (I used to make the same mistake, so I'm trying to help you learn from my errors there).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this would work better as a -J rather than a main article. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the greatest title for a scip, ever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a better version of SCP-1993 in my opinion. I generally don't like CAPs like this, but this is the perfect blend of hilarious and terrifying. Good job overall, but maybe add an experiment log to make it more hilarious and or give insight to the "Family" a bit more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is funny, weird, and unsettling in all the right ways. The note about sexual contact really puts it over the top.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It affects nearly everyone, not hardly anyone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shouldn't it be <1% instead of >99%?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I personally have a preference for the more mundane/less overtly horrifying SCPs and I really like this one for that reason; additionally, I think it wouldn't work nearly so well if it wasn't so serious in tone.

Some SCPs benefit from having joking or casual footnotes, signs that humans work at the Foundation (generally, I find, the ones which are otherwise darker in tone). For something this obviously absurd, to approach it with solemnity does it a big favour and stops it from crossing into -J territory. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah… sorry. I'm actually a fan of Squidbillies (guilty pleasure), so this is, to me, a glaring pop culture reference. Normally I'd downvote something like this, but I get that it was an innocent mistake on your part, so neutral vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This sounds like 168's evil twin.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That sounds like a lot of SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No vote. This is well done as always, but conceptually, it feels like yet another "weird thing that's weird for the sake of being weird."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: With great difficulty, I would imagine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really very very nice. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've never seen an SCP that forces you to deep-throat a calculator before.

So uh…

+1?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think I understand this article. A calculator that chokes you to death unless you've been paid by the company or had sex with someone who was paid by the company?

However, the article is well written and the incredible weirdness of it all was enough to make me upvote. So here, take your +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Whoa. Calculator that prints stuff. Man, that's high-tech.

Interesting concept, although does that even fit in your mouth? I'm confused.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thing what crazes you and kills you to death, only odd enough to make it.

However, I don't think this is a memetic hazard, it's cognitohazardous.

SCP-2061 produces a compulsive effect in 99+% of humans viewing it

If one defines "memetic hazard" as "the hazardous anomalous effects triggered by information acquisition on a sentient being's part, depending or regardless or medium and primer format" and "cognitive hazard" as "the hazardous anomalous effects triggered by perceiving the anomalous primer in any way", 2061 is a cognitive hazard. There is no information codified in the image that can be clearly differentiated from any other part of the sensory input; thus, not memetic.

Also, not sure of this one:

Criteria A

Shouldn't it be either "Criterion" or "Criteria Set A"? May be a stylistic choice, I'm asking out of ignorance.

Upvoted regardless because of the wait, what? effect. That should have its own tag.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't really know how I feel about this one. While I do like the way the article is written, the idea of it all seems a little too silly for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Too late, hilarity ensues

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Real classic feel to this. I think it works because it doesn't crazy you to death necessarily. Also, the transmission vector for immunity is pretty original. It's just a wacky idea played completely straight, which is how you need to do wacky SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Derp.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: James (45), Jennifer (39), Andy (15), Iris (9) and Joseph (4) Madeline

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, last name. Family name/surname.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ugh. I wasn't aware this was an existing thing when you said the idea in chat and I said I'd write it. I want to make clear that I've never seen that scene before writing this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: James (45), Jennifer (39), Andy (15), Iris (9) and Joseph (4) Madeline

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My only problem is the fact that no last name is mentioned in the newspaper.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This. The newspaper was a nice touch too, as was the method of inoculation. Something tells me a member of Sharp really wanted people to buy their shit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just when I think we can't get better titles for these articles.

"Meschk-Mernanschordbtoum-VordhosbnV7"

"The Forensic Ghost of Tupac Shakur"

"I Accidentally the Entire Universe"

And now this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't claim credit for the idea

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kXgd7Gtjpkc

"Was it a EEEVIL calculator?"

"Well why else would it eat the whole family?"

"Err, choked them to death, actually"

"So the WHOLE family choked on ONE calculator?"

….

"You mean like a LIVIN' calculator?"

"I guess it'd have to be for a WHOLE FAMILY to choke on ONE CALCULATOR"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Normally I'm not a fan of compulsory items that make you kill yourself, but the effects on this are just so deliciously bizarre and specific. Plus, the mental image of a family taking turns choking themselves to death on a calculator is horrifyingly hilarious.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I upvoted mainly for the concept that the protection enjoyed by the manufacturer's employees is sexually transmitted. It's the little touches that make a thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Based on an idea by AndarielHalo in chat.

Photo by Joe Haupt, CC-BY.
